sanskrit:
नान्यं गुणेभ्यः कर्तारं यदा द्रष्टानुपश्यति |
गुणेभ्यश्च परं वेत्ति मद्भावं सोऽधिगच्छति ॥१९॥
iast:
nānyaṃ guṇebhyaḥ kartāraṃ yadā draṣṭānupaśyati |
guṇebhyaśca paraṃ vetti madbhāvaṃ so'dhigacchati ||19||
explanation: When the seer perceives no doer other than the gunas and knows That which is beyond the gunas, he attains My nature.
synthesis: When the seer perceives no doer other than the gunas and knows That which is beyond the gunas, he attains My nature. Shankara sees this as the moment of Self-realization — the gunas are seen as belonging to Prakriti, and the Self shines forth as the pure witness. Ramanuja reads it as knowledge of the soul's distinction from Prakriti combined with devotion to God. The Bhakti tradition celebrates the devotee who sees through the gunas to the Lord beyond. Madhva teaches that this requires knowing the soul as witness and God as supreme beyond all gunas. Abhinavagupta interprets this as pratyabhijna — recognizing that all activity belongs to Shakti while consciousness is the unmoved witness, which is knowing oneself as Shiva. Vallabha explains that seeing the gunas as the only doers opens the heart to God's grace and knowing Krishna beyond the gunas is the gateway to liberation. Tilak finds this pivotal: truly seeing the gunas as the doers enables complete freedom and effectiveness in action. Vivekananda sees this as the culmination of self-knowledge — understanding that personality is a play of natural forces while the true Self is the serene observer.

commentaries.text: Shankara identifies this as the direct path to liberation: the seer (drashta) recognizes that all activity belongs to the gunas, not to the self, and simultaneously knows the self as the transcendent witness beyond all gunas. This knowledge is identical with moksha.

Ramanuja explains that the enlightened one sees God as the ultimate agent working through the gunas, and recognizes the Supreme as beyond all material qualities. Attaining 'madbhavam' means achieving a state of being similar to God's — eternal and blissful.

The Bhakti tradition celebrates 'madbhavam' — attaining God's own nature — as the supreme fruit of wisdom combined with devotion. The devotee who sees the gunas as God's play and surrenders completely enters into the Lord's very being through love.

Madhva teaches that the seer who perceives no doer other than the gunas and knows the supreme being beyond the gunas attains God's nature. This seeing requires the knowledge that the soul is a witness, not an actor — and that God is supreme beyond all gunas.

Abhinavagupta interprets this as the moment of pratyabhijna — when the seer recognizes that all activity belongs to Shakti (the gunas) while pure consciousness (Shiva) is the unmoved witness. Knowing 'That which is beyond the gunas' is knowing oneself as Shiva.

Vallabha explains that seeing the gunas as the only doers frees the devotee from ego and opens the heart to God's grace. Knowing the one beyond the gunas means knowing Krishna, who transcends and governs all three gunas. This knowledge is the gateway to liberation.

Tilak finds this verse pivotal for the karma yogi: when you truly see that the gunas alone act and that you are the witness, you can act with complete freedom and effectiveness. This is not passivity but the highest form of engaged detachment.

Vivekananda sees this as the culmination of self-knowledge: understanding that your personality — with all its moods, impulses, and tendencies — is a play of natural forces, while your true Self is the serene observer. This insight transforms your entire relationship with life.

applications.personal_growth: The deepest freedom comes from recognizing that your thoughts, emotions, and impulses are movements of natural forces — not 'you.' When you truly see this, you stop fighting yourself and start witnessing the play of nature from a place of peace.
applications.career_business: When you stop taking credit or blame for outcomes you did not fully control, you paradoxically become more effective. Recognizing the forces at play — market conditions, team dynamics, timing — frees you from ego-driven decisions.
applications.health: Understanding that your body's states are products of natural forces (genetics, environment, habits) rather than personal failures or achievements brings compassion and wisdom to your health journey.
applications.relationships: When you see that people act from the interplay of forces beyond their conscious control, blame dissolves into understanding. This does not mean accepting harmful behavior — it means responding with clarity instead of resentment.

sanskrit:
गुणानेतानतीत्य त्रीन्देही देहसमुद्भवान् |
जन्ममृत्युजरादुःखैर्विमुक्तोऽमृतमश्नुते ॥२०॥
iast:
guṇānetānatītya trīndehī dehasamudbhavān |
janmamṛtyujarāduḥkhairvimukto'mṛtamaśnute ||20||
explanation: The embodied soul, transcending these three gunas which are the source of the body, is freed from birth, death, old age, and sorrow, and attains immortality.
synthesis: Transcending the three gunas that are the source of the body, the embodied soul is freed from birth, death, old age, and suffering, and attains immortality. Shankara sees this as the promise of moksha through knowledge. Ramanuja reads it as liberation through God's grace when the soul rises above Prakriti's influence. The Bhakti tradition celebrates the devotee's ultimate release from all suffering. Madhva explains that the soul rises above Prakriti's influence through God's grace, attaining real freedom from birth and death. Abhinavagupta reads this as consciousness recognizing itself as beyond the gunas — the Self was never born, never ages, never dies — liberation while still alive. Vallabha teaches that transcending the gunas through divine grace is the supreme goal of pushti marga — eternal, blissful communion. Tilak emphasizes that transcendence means acting without being driven by the gunas' compulsions, not ceasing action. Vivekananda sees the promise of immortality as the ultimate potential of every human consciousness.

commentaries.text: Shankara explains that transcending the gunas means recognizing that birth, death, old age, and sorrow belong to the body — a product of the gunas — and not to the self. The self, being ever-free, realizes its own immortal nature (amrita) through knowledge alone.

Ramanuja teaches that when the soul transcends the gunas through knowledge and devotion, it is freed from the cycle of samsara entirely. 'Amritam' refers to the soul's inherent nature of eternality and bliss, fully realized in liberation.

The Bhakti tradition understands 'amritam' as the nectar of eternal divine communion. When the devotee transcends the gunas through surrender and love, they taste the immortality of God's presence — beyond all birth, death, old age, and sorrow.

Madhva explains that transcending the three gunas means the soul rises above Prakriti's influence through God's grace. Freedom from birth, death, old age, and suffering is a real state attained by the soul that correctly understands its own nature and depends entirely on God.

Abhinavagupta reads this as the ultimate promise: consciousness recognizing itself as beyond the gunas attains the nectar of immortality. The gunas are the source of the body, but the Self was never born, never ages, never dies. Recognizing this is liberation while still alive (jivanmukti).

Vallabha teaches that transcending the gunas through divine grace is the supreme goal of pushti marga. The devotee who is lifted beyond sattva, rajas, and tamas by God's mercy enjoys the nectar of immortality — eternal, blissful communion with the Lord.

Tilak emphasizes that transcending the gunas does not mean ceasing all action but acting without being driven by the gunas' compulsions. The liberated karma yogi continues to work in the world but is inwardly free from the forces that bind ordinary people.

Vivekananda sees the promise of immortality as the ultimate potential of human consciousness. Every person has the capacity to rise above the three binding forces and discover the unconditioned freedom that is their true nature. This is the highest human achievement.

applications.personal_growth: Your suffering is not intrinsic to who you are — it belongs to the interplay of forces you can learn to see through. The path to inner freedom is not escaping life but transcending identification with the forces that create suffering.
applications.career_business: Professional burnout, fear of failure, and anxiety about the future all arise from identification with forces outside your true self. The leader who can witness these forces without being consumed by them becomes unbreakable.
applications.health: While the body ages and changes, your relationship to that process can be transformed. Fear of aging, resistance to change, and anxiety about mortality all diminish when you find the part of yourself that is not subject to them.
applications.relationships: The fear of loss that haunts relationships belongs to identification with the temporary. When you touch the eternal within yourself and your loved ones, the quality of your love transforms from desperate clinging to serene cherishing.

sanskrit:
अर्जुन उवाच |
कैर्लिङ्गैस्त्रीन्गुणानेतानतीतो भवति प्रभो |
किमाचारः कथं चैतांस्त्रीन्गुणानतिवर्तते ॥२१॥
iast:
arjuna uvāca |
kairliṅgaistrīnguṇānetānatīto bhavati prabho |
kimācāraḥ kathaṃ caitāṃstrīnguṇānativartate ||21||
explanation: Arjuna said: By what marks is one known who has transcended these three gunas, O Lord? What is their conduct, and how do they pass beyond these three gunas?
synthesis: Arjuna asks: by what marks is one known who has transcended the three gunas? What is their conduct, and how do they transcend? Shankara anticipates the answer will describe the jnani's state of non-identification. Ramanuja expects signs that reveal the soul's devotion to God beyond the gunas. The Bhakti tradition values this question as seeking the portrait of the ideal devotee. Madhva frames it as a sincere seeker's practical inquiry into recognizable signs of transcendence. Abhinavagupta sees it pointing to how liberation manifests in everyday life — transcendence is not escape but a transformed relationship with the gunas. Vallabha appreciates the question because devotion needs concrete signs that inspire the aspirant. Tilak values it because the karma yogi needs practical markers of progress. Vivekananda sees this as the most practical question: what does a free person actually look like — focusing on observable character rather than abstract theology.

commentaries.text: Shankara notes that Arjuna's question is threefold: the signs (linga) by which the transcendent one is recognized, the conduct (achara) they exhibit, and the method (katham) by which they transcend. This comprehensive inquiry ensures a complete teaching.

Ramanuja observes that Arjuna asks both for recognizable characteristics and for the practical path. This combination is essential — knowing what the goal looks like inspires and guides the aspirant, while knowing the method makes it achievable.

The Bhakti tradition values Arjuna's role as the ideal devotee-student who asks precisely the right questions. His sincerity and directness draw out Krishna's most profound and practical teaching about the nature of the liberated being.

Madhva frames Arjuna's question about the marks of one who has transcended the gunas as a sincere seeker's practical inquiry. The devotee needs concrete signs to recognize transcendence both in teachers and as a goal for personal practice.

Abhinavagupta sees Arjuna's question as pointing to a crucial issue: how does liberation manifest in everyday life? The answer will reveal that transcendence is not an escape from the world but a transformed relationship with it — the gunas continue but no longer bind.

Vallabha appreciates Arjuna's question because devotion is never purely abstract — the devotee wants to know how a God-realized person lives, acts, and relates to others. These concrete signs inspire and guide the aspirant on the path of pushti.

Tilak values this question because the karma yogi needs practical markers of progress. How does a person act who has transcended the gunas? What does their conduct look like? These behavioral indicators transform abstract philosophy into a livable ethical program.

Vivekananda sees this as the most practical question possible: forget metaphysics for a moment — what does a free person actually look like? How do they behave? This focus on observable character, not abstract theology, is what makes the Gita universally applicable.

applications.personal_growth: Asking the right question is itself a sign of readiness. When you sincerely ask 'what does real freedom look like and how do I get there?' — you have already begun the journey.
applications.career_business: In any field, progress requires knowing what excellence looks like (signs), how excellent people behave (conduct), and how they got there (method). Seek all three rather than just studying techniques.
applications.health: True wellness is not just the absence of disease. Ask: What does a truly healthy person look like? How do they live? What practices got them there? These questions reframe health from symptom management to genuine vitality.
applications.relationships: To build a great relationship, study great relationships: What are their signs? How do those partners treat each other? What practices sustain their bond? Aspiration needs a model.

sanskrit:
श्रीभगवानुवाच |
प्रकाशं च प्रवृत्तिं च मोहमेव च पाण्डव |
न द्वेष्टि सम्प्रवृत्तानि न निवृत्तानि काङ्क्षति ॥२२॥
iast:
śrībhagavānuvāca |
prakāśaṃ ca pravṛttiṃ ca mohameva ca pāṇḍava |
na dveṣṭi sampravṛttāni na nivṛttāni kāṅkṣati ||22||
explanation: The Blessed Lord said: One who does not hate illumination, activity, or delusion when they arise, O Pandava, nor longs for them when they cease.
synthesis: The Lord says: one who does not hate illumination, activity, or delusion when they arise, nor longs for them when they cease, is said to have transcended the gunas. Shankara sees non-reactivity to all three gunas as the sign of Self-abidance. Ramanuja reads this as the soul resting in God beyond all fluctuations of nature. The Bhakti tradition celebrates this equanimity as the fruit of total surrender to the Lord. Madhva explains that the liberated person recognizes these as natural functions of Prakriti, not disturbances to the soul's real nature resting securely in God. Abhinavagupta teaches that all three gunas are witnessed with equanimity as the spontaneous play of one's own energy. Vallabha sees this non-reactive attitude as the fruit of surrender — the rise and fall of gunas is experienced as God's lila. Tilak describes the ideal worker who works steadily through all psychological weather without preference. Vivekananda interprets this as the highest emotional maturity — accepting each moment's quality without clinging or resistance.

commentaries.text: Shankara explains that the gunatita does not identify with any state of mind — neither resisting darkness when it arises nor clinging to clarity when it appears. This radical non-identification with mental states is the hallmark of abidance in the self.

Ramanuja describes this as a state of complete equanimity toward the effects of the gunas. The wise one recognizes that illumination, activity, and delusion are properties of prakriti and remains unperturbed by their coming and going.

In the Bhakti view, this equanimity arises naturally from total surrender to God. When the devotee has given everything — clarity, activity, and even confusion — to the Lord, there is nothing to hate or crave. Every state becomes an offering.

Madhva explains that the one who has transcended the gunas does not hate illumination, activity, or delusion when they arise because such a person recognizes these as natural functions of Prakriti, not as disturbances to the soul's real nature, which rests securely in God.

Abhinavagupta teaches that the liberated one does not resist any guna when it arises because all three are recognized as movements of Shakti within consciousness. There is no aversion to tamas or clinging to sattva — all are witnessed with equanimity as the spontaneous play of one's own energy.

Vallabha sees this non-reactive attitude as the fruit of surrender to God: when the devotee has offered everything to Krishna, the rise and fall of the gunas is experienced as God's lila. There is nothing to hate and nothing to crave — all is divine play.

Tilak reads this as a description of the ideal worker: one who does not chase sattvic moods, resist rajasic energy, or panic at tamasic states. The guna-transcendent karma yogi works steadily through all psychological weather without preference or aversion.

Vivekananda interprets this as emotional maturity of the highest order: the free person does not fight their own nature or force artificial states. They accept each moment's quality — clarity, activity, or dullness — without clinging or resistance, maintaining inner freedom throughout.

applications.personal_growth: True freedom is not about always being in a peak state — it is about not fighting your current state. When clarity comes, welcome it. When confusion comes, observe it. When restlessness comes, notice it. No resistance, no clinging.
applications.career_business: The resilient professional neither panics in chaos nor becomes complacent in success. They navigate all conditions with the same centered clarity because their identity is not tied to any particular state of business.
applications.health: Real wellness includes the ability to be at peace on bad health days as well as good ones. Not fighting illness, not desperately clinging to peak vitality — just meeting each day's reality with equanimity.
applications.relationships: Mature love does not demand that every moment be blissful. It accepts the full range of relational experience — connection, distance, joy, difficulty — without hating the hard parts or desperately chasing the highs.

उदासीनवदासीनो गुणैर्यो न विचाल्यते |
गुणा वर्तन्त इति एवं योऽवतिष्ठति नेङ्गते ॥२३॥
iast:
udāsīnavadāsīno guṇairyo na vicālyate |
guṇā vartanta iti evaṃ yo'vatiṣṭhati neṅgate ||23||
explanation: One who sits like a witness, undisturbed by the gunas, knowing that the gunas alone act — who remains steady and does not waver.
synthesis: One who sits like a witness, undisturbed by the gunas, knowing that the gunas alone act — who remains firm and unwavering. Shankara identifies this as the state of the sakshi (witness) — pure consciousness observing Prakriti's modifications. Ramanuja sees it as the soul established in its true nature as distinct from Prakriti, devoted to God. The Bhakti tradition recognizes the unwavering devotee who remains centered in God through all conditions. Madhva describes this as serene wisdom grounded in the soul's real relationship with God — witnessing, not passive indifference. Abhinavagupta identifies this witness-consciousness as Shiva's own nature — the unmoving center around which all activity revolves. Vallabha teaches that resting like a witness means resting in God's presence while the gunas play. Tilak emphasizes remaining firm as the key quality — inner stillness amid full engagement in action. Vivekananda sees this as the highest psychological achievement — observing your own states without being swept away.

commentaries.text: Shankara describes the gunatita as established in the knowledge 'the gunas interact with the gunas — I am the witness.' This understanding creates an unshakeable stability that no mental state or external event can disturb.

Ramanuja explains that the wise one understands all activity as the functioning of the gunas of prakriti and remains established in the awareness of the self as distinct from these movements. This creates a stability born of true knowledge.

The Bhakti tradition describes this witness-like state as arising from complete trust in God. The devotee who knows that everything is unfolding according to divine will sits undisturbed, watching the play of the gunas as God's lila.

Madhva describes the gunatita (guna-transcendent) person as sitting like a witness, undisturbed, knowing that the gunas alone act. This witnessing is not passive indifference but a state of serene wisdom grounded in the soul's real relationship with God.

Abhinavagupta identifies this witness-consciousness as Shiva's own nature — the unmoving center around which all activity revolves. The one established in this witnessing does not need to control the gunas; they simply recognize that all movement belongs to Shakti while they rest as pure awareness.

Vallabha teaches that sitting like a witness means resting in God's presence while the gunas play out their drama. The pushti devotee is not detached from emotion but has placed all emotions in God's hands, finding stability not in self-control but in divine support.

Tilak emphasizes 'remaining firm and not wavering' as the key quality: the karma yogi in the midst of action maintains an inner center of stillness. Knowing that the gunas alone act, this person works with full engagement but without inner turbulence.

Vivekananda sees this as the highest psychological achievement: the ability to observe your own mental states — moods, impulses, reactions — without being swept away by them. This witnessing capacity is the foundation of all self-mastery and the key to effective living.

applications.personal_growth: The deepest stability comes from the recognition: 'These forces act, but they are not me.' You can watch your moods, impulses, and reactions as a scientist watches phenomena — with interest but without being swept away.
applications.career_business: The most effective leaders maintain an inner stillness even amid organizational turbulence. They observe market forces, team dynamics, and competitive pressures without losing their center — and from that stillness, they make their best decisions.
applications.health: Witness your body's states without becoming them. Pain is there, but you are not pain. Fatigue is there, but you are not fatigue. This subtle shift from 'I am tired' to 'tiredness is present' changes everything about how you cope.
applications.relationships: In heated moments, the ability to step back and observe — 'anger is arising, not I am angry' — transforms conflict. From the witness position, you respond with wisdom rather than reacting from conditioning.

समदुःखसुखः स्वस्थः समलोष्टाश्मकाञ्चनः |
तुल्यप्रियाप्रियो धीरस्तुल्यनिन्दात्मसंस्तुतिः ॥२४॥
iast:
samaduḥkhasukhaḥ svasthaḥ samaloṣṭāśmakāñcanaḥ |
tulyapriyāpriyo dhīrastulyanindātmasaṃstutiḥ ||24||
explanation: Equal in pleasure and pain, self-contained, regarding a lump of earth, a stone, and gold as equal; the same toward the pleasant and unpleasant, steady, equal in blame and self-praise.
synthesis: Equal in pleasure and pain, self-contained, regarding earth, stone, and gold as equal — the wise one is equal in the pleasant and unpleasant, in praise and blame. Shankara identifies these as the marks of jivanmukti — liberation while embodied. Ramanuja sees this equanimity as the fruit of devotion to God. The Bhakti tradition finds this equipoise in the devotee whose heart is fixed on the Lord alone. Madhva grounds this equanimity in the soul's focus on God rather than worldly valuation. Abhinavagupta sees it as the natural state of consciousness recognizing itself equally in all objects. Vallabha explains it as the fruit of seeing everything as a form of God — all objects are equally sacred. Tilak reads these as practical tests of spiritual maturity for the active person. Vivekananda applies this to everyday character — not emotional flatness but unshakeable inner stability.

commentaries.text: Shankara highlights 'svastha' — established in the self — as the root of all the other qualities. When one is rooted in the infinite, finite distinctions between pleasure and pain, gold and earth, praise and blame become insignificant.

Ramanuja describes this equanimity as arising from the soul's recognition of its own eternal nature. When the self knows itself as distinct from the material body and its experiences, worldly dualities cease to agitate.

The Bhakti tradition sees this equanimity as the natural state of one absorbed in divine love. When the heart is filled with God, worldly distinctions — praise or blame, pleasure or pain, gold or clay — pale into insignificance.

Madhva lists the signs of equanimity: regarding earth, stone, and gold as equal; being the same in pleasant and unpleasant experiences; being steady and equal in praise and blame. This equanimity is grounded in the soul's focus on God rather than on worldly valuation.

Abhinavagupta sees this equanimity as the natural state of consciousness that recognizes itself in all objects equally. When awareness knows itself as the ground of all experience, no particular object — whether gold or earth — can create preferential attachment. All are equally forms of consciousness.

Vallabha explains this equanimity as the fruit of seeing everything as a form of God. When gold, stone, and earth are all recognized as expressions of divine being, worldly valuations lose their power to create attachment or aversion. Everything is equally sacred.

Tilak reads these as practical tests of spiritual maturity for the active person: can you receive praise and criticism with equal composure? Can you handle success and failure without losing your center? These are the marks of a karma yogi who has internalized the teaching of the gunas.

Vivekananda applies this to everyday character: the truly strong person is not one who is indifferent but one who has a center of gravity that external circumstances cannot shift. Equal-mindedness is not emotional flatness but unshakeable inner stability.

applications.personal_growth: True stability comes from being 'svastha' — established in yourself. When your sense of worth comes from within, external praise does not inflate you and criticism does not deflate you. You become unshakeable.
applications.career_business: The leader who is equally composed receiving criticism and praise, facing losses and gains, makes decisions from clarity rather than emotional reaction. This equanimity is not indifference — it is the highest form of engagement.
applications.health: Equanimity toward the body's fluctuating states — pain and comfort, vitality and fatigue — reduces the stress response that amplifies suffering. Accepting what is present without resistance is itself healing.
applications.relationships: When you stop needing your partner to make you happy or fearing they will make you sad, the relationship transforms. Love from self-sufficiency is generous; love from neediness is extractive.

sanskrit:
मानापमानयोस्तुल्यस्तुल्यो मित्रारिपक्षयोः |
सर्वारम्भपरित्यागी गुणातीतः स उच्यते ॥२५॥
iast:
mānāpamānayostulyastulyo mitrāripakṣayoḥ |
sarvārambhaparityāgī guṇātītaḥ sa ucyate ||25||
explanation: Equal in honor and dishonor, equal toward friend and foe, renouncing all self-initiated undertakings — such a one is said to have transcended the gunas.
synthesis: Equal in honor and dishonor, equal toward friend and foe, renouncing all self-initiated undertakings — such a one is said to have transcended the gunas. Shankara sees the renunciation of self-initiated action as the sign of the jnani who rests in the Self. Ramanuja interprets this as the soul's complete reliance on God. The Bhakti tradition celebrates the devotee who has surrendered all personal will to the Lord. Madhva identifies this state as attained through God's grace working through sustained effort and correct understanding. Abhinavagupta teaches that action arises spontaneously from recognition, not from ego-driven planning — the liberated one acts from natural overflow. Vallabha sees this as the complete devotee who has surrendered all personal will to God. Tilak clarifies that renouncing undertakings means renouncing ego-ownership, not ceasing to act. Vivekananda reads this as the portrait of a truly free person — one who acts from inner fullness rather than inner need.

commentaries.text: Shankara clarifies that 'renouncing all undertakings' means abandoning the ego-sense of doership, not ceasing all activity. The wise person may act extensively but without the sense of 'I am doing this for myself.' The ego-motive is renounced, not action itself.

Ramanuja explains that the gunatita treats friend and foe equally because they see the divine in all beings. They renounce self-initiated action because all their activity has become service to God — nothing is undertaken for personal gain.

The Bhakti tradition sees this as the portrait of the perfect devotee: having given everything to God, they have no personal agenda. Honor and dishonor are the same because their identity rests in God, not in others' opinions.

Madhva identifies the guna-transcendent person as one who is equal toward friend and foe, has renounced self-motivated undertakings, and has risen above the gunas. This state is attained through God's grace working through the devotee's sustained effort and correct understanding.

Abhinavagupta teaches that renouncing all self-initiated undertakings means that action arises spontaneously from the recognition of one's nature, not from ego-driven planning. The liberated one still acts but from the natural overflow of consciousness rather than from personal ambition.

Vallabha sees this as the complete devotee: one who has surrendered all personal will to God, treating friend and foe alike because all are seen as expressions of the Lord. This surrender transcends the gunas naturally, without forced asceticism.

Tilak clarifies that 'renouncing all undertakings' means renouncing the ego's ownership of action, not ceasing to act. The karma yogi who has transcended the gunas continues to serve with full energy but without personal stake in outcomes.

Vivekananda reads this as the portrait of a truly free human being: one who has nothing to prove, nothing to defend, and nothing to fear. Such a person acts from inner fullness rather than inner need. This is the highest human possibility.

applications.personal_growth: When your actions are motivated by purpose rather than ego, the outcomes — honor or dishonor, approval or disapproval — no longer determine your inner state. You act because it is right, not because of what you will get.
applications.career_business: The most impactful professionals are those who have moved beyond career management to genuine service. They treat allies and competitors with the same integrity and are not swayed by status or recognition.
applications.health: When health practices are driven by genuine self-care rather than vanity or fear, the results matter less to your emotional state. You do what is right for your body without being enslaved to outcomes.
applications.relationships: Treating all people — friends and difficult individuals alike — with the same fundamental respect is the mark of true maturity. It does not mean equal closeness, but equal dignity and fairness.

sanskrit:
मां च योऽव्यभिचारेण भक्तियोगेन सेवते |
स गुणान्समतीत्यैतान्ब्रह्मभूयाय कल्पते ॥२६॥
iast:
māṃ ca yo'vyabhicāreṇa bhaktiyogena sevate |
sa guṇānsamatītyaitānbrahmabhūyāya kalpate ||26||
explanation: And one who serves Me with unwavering devotion, transcending these gunas, becomes fit for absorption in Brahman.
synthesis: One who serves Me with unwavering devotion, transcending the three gunas, becomes fit for absorption into Brahman. Shankara reads this as devotion leading to the knowledge that culminates in Brahman-realization. Ramanuja sees unwavering devotion as the direct means of transcending the gunas and reaching God. The Bhakti tradition celebrates this as the supreme path — devotion alone transcends all binding forces. Madhva teaches that bhakti is the essential path — through loving service to the Lord the soul rises above Prakriti's forces. Abhinavagupta interprets unwavering devotion as continuous attention toward consciousness's own source, naturally transcending the gunas. Vallabha sees this as the heart of pushti marga — exclusive devotion to Krishna is the only reliable means of transcending the gunas. Tilak reads this as the culmination: the karma yogi must ultimately turn to devotion as the bridge to guna-transcendence. Vivekananda defines this devotion as total dedication to the Highest — through worship, meditation, or service to humanity as the living God.

commentaries.text: Shankara explains that unwavering devotion purifies the mind, enabling the discrimination that leads to Brahman-realization. Bhakti is the means that prepares the intellect for the final knowledge. 'Brahmabhuyaya' indicates becoming one with Brahman.

Ramanuja celebrates this verse as establishing bhakti yoga as the supreme path. Exclusive, unwavering devotion to God naturally transcends all gunas because the devotee is absorbed in the Supreme, who is beyond prakriti. Liberation is the natural result.

This is the culminating verse for the Bhakti tradition in this chapter: all intellectual understanding of the gunas resolves into one practice — serve God with unwavering love. The devotee who does this automatically transcends all three gunas and attains the divine.

Madhva teaches that unwavering devotion to God is the means of transcending the gunas. Bhakti yoga is not merely one option among many but the essential path — it is through loving service to the Lord that the soul rises above Prakriti's binding forces.

Abhinavagupta interprets 'unwavering devotion through yoga' as the continuous attention of consciousness toward its own source — a practice that naturally transcends the gunas because it rests in the awareness that is prior to their manifestation.

Vallabha sees this verse as the heart of pushti marga: exclusive, unwavering devotion to Krishna is the only reliable means of transcending the gunas. Human effort alone cannot overcome the gunas — only God's grace, attracted by devoted love, can accomplish this.

Tilak reads this as the culmination of the chapter's teaching: even the karma yogi, after mastering selfless action, must ultimately turn to devotion. Unwavering service to God through action is the bridge between karma yoga and the transcendence of the gunas.

Vivekananda defines this devotion as total dedication — not necessarily to a personal God but to the Highest, however understood. Whether through worship, meditation, or service to humanity as the living God, unwavering dedication is the path beyond the gunas.

applications.personal_growth: All the analysis of your inner forces ultimately serves one purpose: freeing you to devote yourself wholly to what matters most. Self-knowledge without commitment to something greater than yourself is incomplete.
applications.career_business: Understanding organizational psychology and market forces is valuable, but the truly transformative professional is one who serves a mission with unwavering dedication. Purpose-driven work naturally transcends ego and circumstance.
applications.health: Understanding the gunas of health — clarity, restlessness, and lethargy — is useful, but lasting transformation comes from devotion to the process of wellbeing, not just intellectual understanding of it.
applications.relationships: Unwavering love — consistent, faithful, unconditional — is how you transcend the inevitable ups and downs of any relationship. Analysis helps you understand the dynamics, but devotion is what transforms them.

ब्रह्मणो हि प्रतिष्ठाहममृतस्याव्ययस्य च |
शाश्वतस्य च धर्मस्य सुखस्यैकान्तिकस्य च ॥२७॥
iast:
brahmaṇo hi pratiṣṭhāhamamṛtasyāvyayasya ca |
śāśvatasya ca dharmasya sukhasyaikāntikasya ca ||27||
explanation: For I am the foundation of Brahman — the immortal, the imperishable, the eternal dharma, and absolute bliss.
synthesis: For I am the foundation of Brahman — the immortal, the imperishable, the eternal dharma, and absolute happiness. Shankara reads this as Krishna identifying Himself with the supreme Brahman that is the basis of all. Ramanuja sees God as the ground of both the personal and impersonal aspects of the divine. The Bhakti tradition celebrates this as the ultimate revelation: Krishna is the source of everything, including Brahman. Madhva interprets this as establishing God's supremacy — even the impersonal Brahman rests upon the personal God. Abhinavagupta reads this as Paramashiva declaring Himself the ground of both personal and impersonal divinity. Vallabha sees this as Krishna's ultimate self-revelation: He is the foundation from whom the absolute, eternal dharma, and supreme bliss all flow. Tilak reads this as the philosophical culmination of karma yoga: all duty participates in the cosmic order resting upon God. Vivekananda interprets this as the Gita's declaration that personal and impersonal aspects of divinity are complementary — the same reality approached through knowledge, devotion, or service.

commentaries.text: Shankara interprets 'pratiṣṭhā' as identity: Krishna speaks as Brahman itself, the foundation of all that is immortal, imperishable, and blissful. The personal form is the doorway to the impersonal Absolute — they are ultimately one and the same.

Ramanuja reads this as the definitive statement: the personal God (Narayana/Krishna) is the foundation upon which even Brahman rests. The impersonal Absolute is a manifestation of the personal Supreme. Immortality, dharma, and bliss all find their source in Him.

For the Bhakti tradition, this verse is the crown jewel: Krishna declares that He is the ground of everything — even Brahman, even immortality, even eternal law, even absolute bliss. The highest abstraction has a personal heart, and that heart is God.

Madhva interprets Krishna's declaration 'I am the foundation of Brahman' as establishing God's supremacy: even the impersonal Brahman rests upon the personal God (Vishnu). Immortality, eternal dharma, and absolute happiness all have their source and foundation in the Lord.

Abhinavagupta reads this as Paramashiva declaring Himself the ground of both the personal and impersonal aspects of the divine. The immortal, imperishable dharma and absolute bliss are all attributes of the supreme consciousness that is both the foundation and the fullness of reality.

Vallabha sees this as Krishna's ultimate self-revelation: He is the foundation of Brahman itself — the personal God from whom the impersonal absolute, eternal dharma, and supreme bliss all flow. This confirms that devotion to Krishna is devotion to the ultimate reality.

Tilak reads this as the philosophical culmination of karma yoga: all selfless action is grounded in the eternal dharma that Krishna embodies. The karma yogi's duty is not an arbitrary social obligation but participation in the cosmic order that rests upon God Himself.

Vivekananda interprets this as the Gita's declaration that the personal and impersonal aspects of divinity are not contradictory but complementary. The foundation of all being — the immortal, the imperishable, the absolute — is the same reality approached through knowledge, devotion, or service.

applications.personal_growth: Every value you hold — truth, freedom, purpose, joy — rests on a foundation. This verse invites you to ask: what is the ultimate ground of everything I value? Finding that ground gives everything else meaning and stability.
applications.career_business: Every organization needs a foundation — a core purpose that supports all strategies, goals, and activities. When that foundation is solid and aligned with something greater than profit, everything built upon it endures.
applications.health: The foundation of health is not any single practice but the consciousness that underlies all practices. When your relationship with your own awareness is solid, every health choice flows naturally from that ground.
applications.relationships: The foundation of all love is the recognition of something sacred in the other person. When that foundation is present — a sense of the divine, the eternal, the irreducible worth of the other — the relationship can withstand anything.
